entries.summary: GFDL 1.2 (November 2002) is the version of the Free Documentation License that preceded the Wikipedia-to-CC-BY-SA migration path. It contains no §11 Relicensing clause; derivatives had to remain on the GFDL unless every copyright holder individually agreed to relicense. The FSF archives this version under /licenses/old-licenses/fdl-1.2.html alongside GFDL 1.1, and the FDL 1.3 FAQ confirms that 1.3 (November 2008) was released specifically at the Wikimedia Foundation's request so that 'Wikipedia and similar Wikis using the FDL' could also use CC-BY-SA 3.0. Works released under 'GFDL 1.2 or any later version' automatically gained access to that migration path once 1.3 appeared; works released under 'GFDL 1.2 only' did not.

sources.source: FSF, GNU Free Documentation License 1.2 (canonical text, old-licenses)
sources.excerpt: GNU Free Documentation License Version 1.2, November 2002 Copyright (C) 2000,2001,2002 Free Software Foundation, Inc. ... The purpose of this License is to make a manual, textbook, or other functional and useful document 'free' in the sense of freedom ... This License is a kind of 'copyleft', which means that derivative works of the document must themselves be free in the same sense.

sources.source: FSF, GFDL 1.3 FAQ (why the new minor version was released)
sources.excerpt: Why did you release a new minor version of the FDL? Late last year, the Wikimedia Foundation, which oversees the Wikipedia project, passed a resolution asking us to update the FDL so as to allow Wikipedia and similar Wikis using the FDL to also use the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike (CC BY-SA) 3.0 license. ... The primary change is the addition of section 11. This new provision allows certain materials released under this license to also be used under the terms of CC BY-SA 3.0. ... If the work was released under the terms of 'the GNU Free Documentation License, version 1.2 or (at your option) any later version,' then it meets this criteria.

This page contains old versions of the licenses published by the Free Software Foundation, kept here for reference. ... Old versions of the GNU Free Documentation License: GNU Free Documentation License, version 1.2; GNU Free Documentation License, version 1.1.

entries.summary: GFDL 1.2 allows authors to mark certain sections (Invariant Sections, Cover Texts, Acknowledgements, Dedications) as non-modifiable and non-removable, typically to preserve political statements such as the GNU Manifesto within GNU manuals. This feature was carried over unchanged into GFDL 1.3. It is controversial within the free-software community: in Debian's 2006 General Resolution (vote_001), the project concluded that works with Invariant Sections conflict with the DFSG's modifiability requirement and are unsuitable for Debian main, while GFDL works without Invariant Sections are DFSG-free. The FSF's license list continues to recommend the GFDL for copylefted free documentation.

sources.source: Debian, General Resolution: Why the GNU Free Documentation License is not suitable for Debian main (vote_001, 2006)
sources.excerpt: [GFDL] allows for non-removable, non-modifiable parts to be present in documents licensed under it. Such parts are commonly referred to as 'invariant sections', and are described in Section 4 of the GFDL. As modifiability is a fundamental requirement of the Debian Free Software Guidelines, this restriction is not acceptable for us, and we cannot accept in our distribution works that include such unmodifiable content. ... At the same time, we also consider that works licensed under the GNU Free Documentation License that include no invariant sections do fully meet the requirements of the Debian Free Software Guidelines.

GNU Free Documentation License (#FDL) This is a license intended for use on copylefted free documentation. We plan to adopt it for all GNU manuals. It is also suitable for other kinds of useful works (such as textbooks and dictionaries, for instance). Its applicability is not limited to textual works.

sources.source: Debian, General Resolution: Why the GNU Free Documentation License is not suitable for Debian main
